hero.title: Keep your fleet checked, documented and ready to roll
hero.description: Fleecheck helps SMB teams centralize vehicle inspections, photo evidence and follow-up actions in one simple workflow shared with the field.
hero.card_description: Readable checklists that are fast to complete and easy to review.
panel_points: Photo evidence attached to each checked point
A clear history so teams can act before small issues waste time

proofs.label: Photos and notes in one place
Inspection history that stays easy to find

landing.benefits_title: A simple structure for better field follow-up
landing.benefits_description: Fleecheck does not replace your operations. It makes them visible, consistent and usable.

benefits.title: Standardize inspections
benefits.description: Every vehicle follows the same checklist so teams miss less and keep a consistent quality bar.

benefits.title: Document with evidence
benefits.description: Photos, notes and answers stay attached to the checklist so the real state of the vehicle is easy to review.

benefits.title: Keep follow-up under control
benefits.description: Inspection history helps the team prioritize actions before a blocking issue appears in the field.

landing.steps_title: A workflow the whole team can read
landing.steps_description: Create your checklists, assign them to the fleet and track feedback without a parallel spreadsheet.

steps.title: Prepare templates
steps.description: Define the control points that match your vehicles and your field routines.

steps.title: Run inspections
steps.description: Field teams complete the checklist, add photos and report remarks at the right moment.

steps.title: Drive follow-up
steps.description: Review history, spot recurring issues and organize the next actions with more clarity.

landing.features_title: Product foundations highlighted on the landing page
landing.features_description: The landing page presents the scope already visible in the product, without promising more than what exists.

features.title: Checklist templates
features.description: Create repeatable inspections by vehicle type or operating routine.

features.title: Photo evidence and remarks
features.description: Capture the right context during inspection so follow-up conversations stay concrete.

features.title: Execution history
features.description: Keep a simple view of past inspections and the points that still need attention.

features.title: Clear sharing across the team
features.description: Give the same frame to the people who execute, review and monitor vehicle checklists.

cta.title: Move from scattered follow-up to an actionable checklist flow
cta.description: Create your workspace, organize your fleet and start your first inspections with a clear baseline.

footer.description: Fleecheck helps teams keep a fleet checked, documented and ready to move.
footer.note: Available in the four languages already handled by the application.

index.page_description: Complete guide to install, configure and use the boilerplate.

hero.title: Boilerplate documentation
hero.description: This page centralizes installation, configuration, architecture, key helpers and the boilerplate update workflow.

summary_cards.title: Quick start
summary_cards.description: Clone the repository, configure `.env`, generate Prisma and run `bun dev`.

summary_cards.title: Custom code
summary_cards.description: Put your business logic in `src/project/*` so it stays protected during upstream merges.

summary_cards.title: Security
summary_cards.description: Use `src/lib/access-control.js` for all authentication, role and permission checks.

summary_cards.title: Internationalization
summary_cards.description: Every new user-facing string must go through `next-intl` and exist in all four locales.

commands.description: Starts the development server and runs `prisma generate --no-hints` before Next.js.
Builds the application for production with Prisma regeneration.
Runs `prisma migrate deploy` and then starts Next.js in production mode.
Runs `biome check .` to validate JavaScript, JSX, JSON and formatting.
Applies Biome formatting across the whole repository.
Opens Prisma Studio to inspect local data.
Listens to local Stripe webhooks and forwards them to `/api/auth/stripe/webhook`.

overview.title: What the boilerplate includes
overview.description: The repository provides a SaaS-ready foundation with authentication, organizations, billing, transactional email and an admin interface already wired in.
paragraphs: The current stack is built on Next.js 16 with the App Router, React 19, Prisma 7, Better Auth, Tailwind CSS 4, shadcn/ui, Stripe, Resend and next-intl.
The goal is to add your business logic in the protected project areas while continuing to pull boilerplate improvements through `git merge upstream/main`.
bullets: Do not edit `src/components/ui/*`: those shadcn/ui wrappers are managed by the boilerplate.
Put project-specific code in `src/project/*` so it stays protected during upstream updates.
Use `src/lib/access-control.js` for all authentication, role and permission checks.
Store all user-facing text in `src/messages/*.json` or `*.project.json`.
Run `bun lint` after every change before you finish.
overview.note: The source of truth is the repository code itself, especially `package.json`, `src/lib/*`, `src/actions/*` and the Prisma files.

installation.title: Install a new project
installation.description: You can either use the setup script for a fast bootstrap or follow the manual process if you want full control over each step.
paragraphs: Before starting the app, prepare Git, Bun, a PostgreSQL database and an empty Git repository for your final project.
The `./scripts/setup-new-project.sh` script configures `origin` and `upstream`, enables `merge=theirs` for protected files and updates `src/site-config.js`.
bullets: Git must be installed and configured.
Bun must be available locally.
A PostgreSQL database must be ready for development.
Create an empty Git repository for the final project.
Copy `.env.example` to `.env` before the first boot.

commands.title: Useful scripts
commands.description: The `package.json` scripts cover startup, code quality, Prisma tasks and Stripe webhooks.
paragraphs: In development, `bun dev` restarts Next.js and regenerates Prisma before the server boots.
For production, `bun start` runs `prisma migrate deploy` before starting Next.js.
bullets: Use `bun dev` for local development.
Use `bun build` to validate the production build.
Use `bun lint` and `bun format` for code quality.
Use `bun studio` and `bun db:seed` to inspect and populate the database.

configuration.title: Configure the environment
configuration.description: Initial setup focuses on environment variables, branding and external services.
paragraphs: Start from `.env.example`, then customize `src/site-config.js` with the project name, description, URLs, sender email and organization options.
The boilerplate Prisma schema lives in `prisma/schema/base.prisma`; your business models must go into `prisma/schema/project.prisma`.
bullets: `.env` for database access, Better Auth, Resend, Stripe and S3 storage.
`src/site-config.js` for branding, app identifier, emails and organization limits.
`public/images/logo.png` and `public/images/icon.png` for visual identity.
`prisma/schema/project.prisma` for your project-specific Prisma models.
`src/messages/*.project.json` for your project translations.

structure.title: Where to put your code
structure.description: The repository clearly separates what belongs to the boilerplate from what belongs to your project.
paragraphs: `src/actions`, `src/components`, `src/hooks`, `src/lib` and a large part of `src/app` are updated from upstream.
Your main customization area is `src/project/`, plus `prisma/schema/project.prisma`, `*.project.json`, `src/app/page.js`, `src/app/globals.css` and `src/site-config.js`.
bullets: `src/project/components/` for your business components.
`src/project/actions/*.action.js` for project-specific Server Actions.
`src/project/hooks/` and `src/project/lib/` for hooks and utilities.
`src/project/features/` for feature-oriented modules.
`src/project/sidebar/` for application navigation.
Use `@/` for boilerplate code and `@project/` for protected project code.

access-control.title: Authentication and access control
access-control.description: Application access is centralized so that checks stay consistent and are not duplicated across the codebase.
paragraphs: To protect a server page, use `requireAuth`, `requireActiveOrganization`, `requirePermission` or `requireAdmin` from `src/lib/access-control.js`.
For conditional UI, prefer `checkPermission` and `checkAdmin`, which return booleans instead of throwing `notFound()`.
bullets: `requireAuth()` returns `{ session, user }` and blocks anonymous access.
`requireActiveOrganization()` adds the active organization to the response.
`requirePermission({ permissions })` validates Better Auth permissions on the active organization.
`requireAdmin()` protects global admin screens.
`checkPermission()` and `checkAdmin()` are meant for conditional rendering.
access-control.note: Avoid manual checks like `user.role === "admin"` or direct `auth.api.getSession()` calls in pages.

organizations.title: Organization management and permissions
organizations.description: Organizations, members, invitations and roles are already connected to Better Auth Organization.
paragraphs: The session stores `activeOrganizationId` and the actions in `src/actions/organization.action.js` drive creation, selection, invitations and deletion.

The base permission set includes `app.use`, `organization.update/delete`, `member.create/update/delete/read`, `invitation.read/create/cancel` and `billing.manage`, and can be extended from `src/project/add-on-organization-permissions.js`.
bullets: Organization creation with automatic slug generation.
Automatic activation of the new organization after creation.
Member invitations by email with `member` or `admin` role.
Role changes, member removal and invitation cancellation.
Different permission matrices for `owner`, `admin` and `member`.

server-actions.title: Server actions, forms and confirmations
server-actions.description: The boilerplate separates server-side mutations from client UI and provides a shared wrapper for pending state and toast handling.
paragraphs: Declare mutations in a `.action.js` file with `'use server'`, then call them from client components through `useServerAction()`.
For forms, the existing pattern combines React Hook Form, Zod and the components from `@/components/ui/form`.
bullets: Define Zod schemas inside the client component when validation messages depend on `useTranslations()`.
Call `execute(() => myAction(values), { successMessage, redirectOnSuccess, refreshOnSuccess })`.
Use `dialogManager.confirm(...)` for destructive flows that already exist in the repository.
Keep client components small and prefer data loading in Server Components when possible.

billing-email.title: Billing, plans and transactional email
billing-email.description: Stripe and Resend are already integrated for billing, subscription plans and transactional email.
paragraphs: Plans are stored in Prisma and enriched with Stripe price data in `src/actions/subscription.action.js`.
Verification and invitation emails are sent through Resend from `src/actions/email.action.js`, using branding from `src/site-config.js`.
bullets: Use `bun stripe-webhooks` to listen to Stripe webhooks locally.
`createCheckoutSession()` opens a secure checkout from a `planId`.
`createBillingPortalSession()` redirects to the Stripe customer portal.
Subscription plans are managed in `src/actions/plan.action.js` and the related admin screens.
Emails rely on `RESEND_API_KEY`, `EMAIL_FROM` and the React Email templates in `src/components/email/`.

i18n.title: Translations and language switching
i18n.description: The project uses next-intl on both the server and the client, with a `NEXT_LOCALE` cookie to remember the selected language.
paragraphs: The currently supported locales are `fr-FR`, `en-US`, `nl-NL` and `de-DE`. The current default locale in code is `en-US` in `src/lib/i18n/config.js`.
Boilerplate messages live in `src/messages/*.json`, while your business translations should live in `src/messages/*.project.json`.
bullets: Use `getTranslations()` in Server Components and `useTranslations()` in Client Components.
Add every new key to all four base locales.
Translate dynamic labels from business keys, for example `tRoles(member.role)`.
Use `setLocaleAction({ locale })` to persist the user's choice.
i18n.note: If you want French as the default language, change `defaultLocale` in `src/lib/i18n/config.js`.

updates.title: Pull new boilerplate changes
updates.description: The update workflow is designed to bring in boilerplate changes quickly without overwriting your project code.
paragraphs: After `git fetch upstream`, merge `upstream/main` and reinstall dependencies if needed.
Protected areas stay local thanks to `.gitattributes` and the `merge=theirs` strategy, but `package.json` can still require a manual merge if both branches changed it.
bullets: Run `bun install` if dependencies changed.
Run `bun prisma generate` if the schema changed.
Check `package.json`, environment variables and pending migrations.
Finish with `bun lint` and ideally `bun build`.

quota.at_limit_description: The organization already uses {used} vehicle(s) for {limit} license(s). Existing vehicles can still be managed, but no new vehicle can be added until the subscription is adjusted.
quota.over_limit_description: The organization uses {used} vehicle(s) for {limit} license(s). Charroi dashboard mutations for vehicles, public links, and checklists are locked until the subscription is regularized. Public checklists remain available.
quota.vehicle_quota_reached_error: The vehicle quota is reached ({used}/{limit}). Increase the number of licenses before adding a new vehicle.
quota.dashboard_locked_error: The vehicle quota is exceeded ({used}/{limit}). The Charroi dashboard is locked until the subscription is regularized.
